subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 4! In this unit you will learn how to describe a whole set of numbers with a single, sensible value. That single value is called an average. When Amina wants to know the *typical* height of the children in her class, when a fruit seller in the Hargeisa market wants to know how many bananas a bunch *usually* holds, or when a football coach in Mogadishu wants to describe how his team *normally* scores, they are all reaching for the same idea: one number that stands for the whole group.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Explain what an average is and why averages help us understand data.
Find the mode of a set of data – the value that appears most often – for both numbers and categories.
Recognise data sets with no mode or with more than one mode.
Find the median by arranging the data in order and locating the middle value.
Find the median when there is an even number of values by averaging the two middle numbers.
Calculate the mean by adding all the values and dividing by how many there are.
Find the range of a set of data by subtracting the smallest value from the largest.
Choose and justify which average is most useful for a particular situation.
Interpret averages in real-world problems and explain what your answer means.

concepts.title: What Is an Average?
concepts.explanation: Imagine you and your friends are comparing how tall you are. Some are taller, some are shorter. If someone asked, “How tall are the children in your group?” you could read out every single height – but that is a long, confusing answer. Instead, you can give one number that represents the whole group. That number is an average. An average is a way of finding the “typical” or “middle” value of a set of data. It is like a spokesperson for the whole group: one value that speaks for all of them. The clever part is that…
concepts.example: Imagine you and your friends are comparing how tall you are. Some are taller, some are shorter. If someone asked, “How tall are the children in your group?” you could read out every single height – but that is a long,…

concepts.title: The Mode
concepts.explanation: Here is the great thing about the mode: you do not need to put the numbers in order first. You simply count how many times each value appears, and the one with the highest count wins. Let us walk through an example step by step. Example: Find the mode of these test scores: 5, 3, 7, 5, 2, 5, 8.
concepts.example: Count how often each number appears. 5 appears 3 times; 3 appears once; 7 appears once; 2 appears once; 8 appears once.

concepts.title: The Median
concepts.explanation: The median is the middle value when you arrange all the numbers in order from smallest to largest. Picture your friends lining up from shortest to tallest: the person standing in the exact middle of the line represents the median height. The median does not care how big or small the numbers are – it only cares about *position*. There is one golden rule for the median that you must never forget: always put the numbers in order first. This is the most common place learners slip up. If the numbers are jumbled, the…
concepts.example: Finding the median with an odd number of values

concepts.title: The Mean
concepts.explanation: The mean is the average most people think of first. To find the mean, you add up all the values and then divide by how many values there are. The mean shares the total out equally among all the data points, as if you gathered everything into one big pile and then split it fairly. Here is a picture that makes the mean clear. Imagine five children have collected dates for iftar: 3, 5, 4, 6, and 2 dates. If they poured all their dates into one bowl and shared them out equally, how many would each child get? First…
concepts.example: Example: Find the mean of these football scores: 2, 1, 3, 2, 2.

concepts.title: The Range
concepts.explanation: The range is not an average – it does not tell you the typical or middle value. Instead, the range tells you how spread out the data is. To find the range, you subtract the smallest value from the largest value. A small range means the numbers are close together; a large range means they are far apart. Example: A group of camels drink these amounts of water in litres: 8, 12, 9, 15, 11. The largest value is 15 and the smallest is 8, so the range is 15 − 8 = 7 litres. This tells us that from the thirstiest camel to…
concepts.example: Why is the range useful? Because two data sets can have the *same* average but be very different. Suppose two football teams both have a mean of 3 goals per match. Team A scored 3, 3, 2, 4, 3 – very steady, with a range…

concepts.title: Choosing and Interpreting the Right Average
concepts.explanation: Now that you know the mode, median, mean and range, the real skill is deciding which to use and understanding what your answer means. Different averages can give different insights about the very same data, so a good mathematician always asks: “What is this data trying to tell me, and which tool answers my question best?” Use the mode when you want to know what happens most often, or when you are dealing with categories such as colours, sizes or types. A shoe shop in Eastleigh choosing which size to stock most of…
concepts.example: Use the median when you want the middle value and there are a few unusually high or low numbers that might mislead you. A bank describing a “typical” account balance uses the median so that a handful of very rich…

methods.title: How to find the mode
steps: Count how many times each value appears (a tally helps).
Find the value with the highest count.
If two or more tie, they are all modes. If none repeats, there is no mode.

methods.title: How to find the median
methods.example: Find the median of these heights: 142, 138, 145, 140, 143.
steps: Arrange all the numbers from smallest to largest.
If there is an odd number of values, the middle one is the median.
If there is an even number, add the two middle values and divide by 2.

methods.title: How to find the mean
steps: Add up every value to get the total.
Count how many values there are.
Divide the total by the count.

methods.title: How to find the range
methods.example: Find the mean of these daily temperatures in °C: 22, 25, 23, 28, 24, 26, 23.
steps: Find the largest value and the smallest value.
Subtract the smallest from the largest.
Write the answer as a single number.

methods.title: Find the range
methods.example: Find the range of these pizza sales: 45, 52, 45, 38, 67, 45, 41.
steps: Solution: The largest value is 67 and the smallest is 38.
Range = 67 − 38 = 29 pizzas.
This tells us the busiest day sold 29 more pizzas than the quietest day.

methods.title: All four measures at once
methods.example: For the scores 3, 7, 3, 9, 5, 3, 8, find the mode, median, mean and range.
steps: Solution: Mode: 3 appears 3 times, more than any other, so the mode is 3.
Median: order them – 3, 3, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9; there are 7 values, so the middle (4th) is 5.
Mean: 3 + 7 + 3 + 9 + 5 + 3 + 8 = 38, and 38 ÷ 7 = 5.43 (to two decimal places).
Range: 9 − 3 = 6.

workedExamples.title: Find the mode
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count each value. 8 appears 4 times; 5 appears twice; 3 once; 7 once. The value with the highest count is 8. The mode is 8. Notice we did not need to sort the numbers at all – only count.

workedExamples.title: Find the median (odd number of values)
workedExamples.prompt: Find the median of these heights: 142, 138, 145, 140, 143.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First arrange in order: 138, 140, 142, 143, 145. There are 5 values, so the middle is the 3rd. Crossing from both ends leaves 142. The median is 142 cm.

workedExamples.title: Find the median (even number of values)
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Order them: 15, 18, 22, 25. With 4 values, the two middle numbers are 18 and 22. Average them: (18 + 22) ÷ 2 = 40 ÷ 2 = 20. The median is 20.

workedExamples.title: Find the mean
workedExamples.prompt: Find the mean of these daily temperatures in °C: 22, 25, 23, 28, 24, 26, 23.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add them all: 22 + 25 + 23 + 28 + 24 + 26 + 23 = 171. Count the values: there are 7. Divide: 171 ÷ 7 = 24.43°C (to two decimal places). So a typical day was a little over 24°C.

workedExamples.title: Find the range
workedExamples.prompt: Find the range of these pizza sales: 45, 52, 45, 38, 67, 45, 41.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The largest value is 67 and the smallest is 38. Range = 67 − 38 = 29 pizzas. This tells us the busiest day sold 29 more pizzas than the quietest day.

workedExamples.title: All four measures at once
workedExamples.prompt: For the scores 3, 7, 3, 9, 5, 3, 8, find the mode, median, mean and range.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Mode: 3 appears 3 times, more than any other, so the mode is 3. Median: order them – 3, 3, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9; there are 7 values, so the middle (4th) is 5. Mean: 3 + 7 + 3 + 9 + 5 + 3 + 8 = 38, and 38 ÷ 7 = 5.43 (to two decimal places). Range: 9 − 3 = 6. So mode = 3, median = 5, mean ≈ 5.43, range = 6.

workedExamples.title: Choosing the right average
workedExamples.prompt: A shoe store sold these sizes: 6, 7, 7, 8, 7, 9, 7, 6, 7, 10. The manager can only reorder one size. Which average should guide the decision, and what is it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The manager wants the size that sells *most often*, so the mode is the right tool. Counting: size 7 appears 5 times, more than any other. The mode is size 7, so the manager should reorder size 7. Here the mode is far more useful than the mean or median, because the question is about popularity, not a middle value.

workedExamples.title: Working backwards from the mean
workedExamples.prompt: Omar scored a mean of 8 marks across 4 quizzes. His first three scores were 7, 9 and 8. What did he score on the fourth quiz?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: If the mean of 4 quizzes is 8, then the total must be 8 × 4 = 32 marks. His first three scores add to 7 + 9 + 8 = 24. So the fourth score is 32 − 24 = 8 marks. Working backwards from the mean like this is a powerful problem-solving skill.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Find the mode of these pet types: Dog, Cat, Dog, Bird, Dog, Cat, Fish, Dog.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A basketball team scored 65, 72, 68, 75, 70 points. Find the mean and the range of their scores.

workedExamples.title: Which set has NO mode
workedExamples.prompt: Which set has NO mode: (a) 2, 2, 3, 4 or (b) 5, 6, 7, 8? Explain.
workedExamples.solution: Set (b) 5,6,7,8 has no mode because every value appears once; set (a) has mode 2.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Create a set of 5 numbers where the mode is 8 and the median is 8.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Example: 8, 8, 8, 9, 10 (8 is most common and is the middle value). Other answers possible – you need at least two 8s and 8 in the middle position.

activities.title: Mode Detective Around the House
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and your eyes.
steps: Choose something to count around your home – the colours of cups, the sizes of shoes by the door, or the types of fruit in the kitchen.
Make a tally as you count each type, for example: red |||, blue ||||, green |.
Find the mode – the type that appears most often.
Now find something that has no mode (every type appears the same number of times). Can you? Write down what you found.
Write one sentence: “The mode of ___ in my home is ___.” Share it with your family.

activities.title: The Median Line-Up
activities.materials: five or more family members or friends, and a tape measure or ruler (optional).
steps: Ask several people to line up from shortest to tallest.
The person standing exactly in the middle shows the median height – point to them!
If there is an even number of people, the median is halfway between the two people in the middle.
Try again using ages instead of heights. Who is the median age in your family?
This activity shows why we always put data ‘in order’ before finding the median – you are literally lining the data up.

activities.title: The Fair-Share Mean Game
activities.materials: a handful of small objects (dried beans, stones, or bottle caps) and a few small bowls or cups.
steps: Put a different number of beans into each of 4 or 5 cups – for example 3, 6, 2 and 5 beans.
Now pour all the beans together and count the total.
Share the beans out equally among the cups again. How many does each cup get?
That fair-share number IS the mean! Check it by adding your starting numbers and dividing by the number of cups.
Try it several times with different amounts. You will feel in your hands what ‘the mean’ really means: sharing the total out equally.

activities.title: Weather Data Investigation
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a way to find daily temperatures (a phone with adult help, a newspaper, or a thermometer).
steps: Record the daily high temperature for 7 days in a row.
Find the mode, median, mean and range of your week's temperatures.
Draw a simple chart with one bar for each day.
Write a short ‘weather report’: “This week the mean temperature was ___°C and the range was ___°C, so the days were mostly ___.”

activities.title: Family Survey Project
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and at least 6 family members or friends to ask.
steps: Choose a fun question, such as “How many hours did you sleep last night?” or “How many cups of tea do you drink a day?”
Collect a number from each person and write them all down.
Find the mode, median and mean of your data.
Decide which average best describes your family, and explain your choice in one or two sentences.
Present your findings as a colourful ‘family data report’ that a younger child could understand.

activities.title: Averages Puzzle Challenge
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and your thinking cap!
steps: Puzzle 1: I am thinking of 5 numbers. Their median is 12 and their mode is 8. Find at least two different sets that work.
Puzzle 2: A gardener measures plant heights in cm: 15, 12, 18, 15, 20, 15, 14. Find the mode, median, mean and range. Then, if one more plant grows to 16 cm, work out how each measure changes.
Puzzle 3: Make two different data sets that have the SAME mode but DIFFERENT medians. Write both sets and label the mode and median of each.
Puzzle 4: A team scored 45, 52, 45, 48, 45, 50, 47 points. The coach says their ‘average’ is 45. Which average is the coach using – mode, median or mean? Work out all three to decide.

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which activity helped you understand averages best? When would you use the mode in real life, and when would you use the mean? What is the difference between an average and the range? What was the trickiest part, and how did you work through it? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: The Mode The mode is the value that appears MOST OFTEN. Just count each value – you do NOT need to put them in order. A set can have no mode, one mode, or several modes.

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: The Median The median is the MIDDLE value once the data is in order. Always arrange smallest to largest FIRST. For an even number of values, average the two middle numbers.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: The Mean Mean = total of all values divided by how many values there are. Add them all up, then divide. The mean can be a decimal and need not match any value in the list.

rules.title: Key rule 4
rules.text: The Range Range = largest value minus smallest value. It measures SPREAD, not the average. Give one number, not a pair – do the subtraction.

terms: A single value representing a whole set of data
The value that appears most often
The middle value when data is in order
Total of the values divided by how many there are
commonMistakes: Just count how often each value appears
Forgetting to divide for the mean
Divide the total by the number of values
Giving the range as two numbers
Ignoring the two middle numbers (even set)

realProblems.prompt: A movie theatre in Mombasa collected these ratings for a new film: 7, 8, 6, 9, 7, 8, 7, 10, 7, 6. Find the mode, median and mean. Which average should appear in the advertisement, and why?
realProblems.answer: 1) Mode = 7; ordered 6,6,7,7,7,7,8,8,9,10 so median = 7; mean = 75 ÷ 10 = 7.5. Advertisers might use the mean (7.5) as it sounds highest, though the mode and median (7) are also fair.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Mode = 7; ordered 6,6,7,7,7,7,8,8,9,10 so median = 7; mean = 75 ÷ 10 = 7.5. Advertisers might use the mean (7.5) as it sounds highest, though the mode and median (7) are also fair.

realProblems.prompt: Daily high temperatures in Hargeisa for a week were 22, 25, 23, 28, 24, 26, 23 (°C). Find the mean and the range. Describe the week's weather in a sentence using both.
realProblems.answer: Mean = 171 ÷ 7 = 24.43°C; range = 28 − 22 = 6°C. The week was warm, averaging about 24°C, with only a 6° gap between the coolest and warmest days – so it was fairly steady.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Mean = 171 ÷ 7 = 24.43°C; range = 28 − 22 = 6°C. The week was warm, averaging about 24°C, with only a 6° gap between the coolest and warmest days – so it was fairly steady.

realProblems.prompt: The number of siblings of children in a class: 0, 1, 1, 2, 1, 3, 1, 0, 2, 1, 4, 1. Find the mode and median. If a new student asks “How many siblings do most children have?”, which average answers the question?
realProblems.answer: Mode = 1 (appears 6 times); ordered the 12 values are 0,0,1,1,1,1,1,1,2,2,3,4, so the two middle values (6th and 7th) are both 1, giving median = 1. The mode (1) answers “most children”.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Mode = 1 (appears 6 times); ordered the 12 values are 0,0,1,1,1,1,1,1,2,2,3,4, so the two middle values (6th and 7th) are both 1, giving median = 1. The mode (1) answers “most children”.

realProblems.prompt: Two market stalls sell mangoes. Stall A's daily sales are 20, 22, 21, 19, 23; Stall B's are 5, 40, 10, 35, 15. Find the mean and range of each. Both have the same mean – which stall is more reliable, and how does the range show this?
realProblems.answer: Stall A: mean = 105 ÷ 5 = 21, range = 23 − 19 = 4. Stall B: mean = 105 ÷ 5 = 21, range = 40 − 5 = 35. Same mean, but Stall A's small range (4) shows steady, reliable sales, while Stall B's large range (35) shows wildly changing sales.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Stall A: mean = 105 ÷ 5 = 21, range = 23 − 19 = 4. Stall B: mean = 105 ÷ 5 = 21, range = 40 − 5 = 35. Same mean, but Stall A's small range (4) shows steady, reliable sales, while Stall B's large range (35) shows wildly changing sales.

realProblems.prompt: During Eid, Zahra gives sweets to children. She records how many each child takes: 2, 3, 2, 4, 2, 3, 2, 5. Find the mode, median and mean, and say which best describes a typical child's share.
realProblems.answer: Ordered: 2,2,2,2,3,3,4,5. Mode = 2; median = (2+3) ÷ 2 = 2.5; mean = 23 ÷ 8 = 2.875. The mode (2) best describes a typical child's share, since 2 sweets is by far the most common.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Ordered: 2,2,2,2,3,3,4,5. Mode = 2; median = (2+3) ÷ 2 = 2.5; mean = 23 ÷ 8 = 2.875. The mode (2) best describes a typical child's share, since 2 sweets is by far the most common.

realProblems.answer: 1) Example: 8, 8, 8, 9, 10 (8 is most common and is the middle value). Other answers possible – you need at least two 8s and 8 in the middle position.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Example: 8, 8, 8, 9, 10 (8 is most common and is the middle value). Other answers possible – you need at least two 8s and 8 in the middle position.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Create a set of 5 numbers where the mode is 8 and the median is 8.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) Example: 8, 8, 8, 9, 10 (8 is most common and is the middle value). Other answers possible – you need at least two 8s and 8 in the middle position.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: The mean of 6 numbers is 9. Five of them are 7, 8, 10, 11 and 9. Find the sixth number.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Total must be 9 × 6 = 54; the five given add to 45; sixth number = 54 − 45 = 9.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Sarah says: “My set of numbers has a mode of 12 and a median of 15.” Could this be true? If yes, give an example; if no, explain why not.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Yes, it can be true. Example: 10, 12, 12, 15, 20 – the mode is 12 (appears twice) and the median is 15 (the middle value). The mode does not have to be the middle number.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A company says its employees' mean salary is high, but most workers earn far less while a few managers earn a great deal. Which average – mean or median – gives a more honest picture of a typical worker's pay? Explain.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The median gives a more honest picture, because the few very high manager salaries pull the mean upward, while the median shows what a middle worker actually earns.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in The Range.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The range is not an average – it does not tell you the typical or middle value. Instead, the range tells you how spread out the data is. To find the range, you subtract the smallest value from the largest value. A small range means the numbers are close together; a large range means they are far apart. Example: A group of camels drink these amounts of water in litres: 8, 12, 9, 15, 11. The largest value is 15 and the smallest is 8, so the range is 15 − 8 = 7 litres. This tells us that from the thirstiest camel to…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Choosing and Interpreting the Right Average.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Now that you know the mode, median, mean and range, the real skill is deciding which to use and understanding what your answer means. Different averages can give different insights about the very same data, so a good mathematician always asks: “What is this data trying to tell me, and which tool answers my question best?” Use the mode when you want to know what happens most often, or when you are dealing with categories such as colours, sizes or types. A shoe shop in Eastleigh choosing which size to stock most of…

selfAssessment: I can explain what an average is and why averages help us understand data.
I can find the mode of a set of data – the value that appears most often – for both numbers and categories.
I can recognise data sets with no mode or with more than one mode.
I can find the median by arranging the data in order and locating the middle value.
I can find the median when there is an even number of values by averaging the two middle numbers.
I can calculate the mean by adding all the values and dividing by how many there are.
I can find the range of a set of data by subtracting the smallest value from the largest.
I can choose and justify which average is most useful for a particular situation.
